regime_classification.2026-04-09:policy: {"run_date":"2026-04-09","dimension":"policy","metric_summary":"Repo 5.25%%","signal_text":"Repo at 5.25% with CPI at 3.21% implies a real rate of approximately 200bps — restrictive enough to justify a cut, but Brent at $98 and food inflation accelerating to 3.47% give the MPC legitimate cover to wait. regime_classification.2026-04-09:capex: {"run_date":"2026-04-09","dimension":"capex","metric_summary":"IIP CapG 12.5%%; CU Not available in search results%","signal_text":"IIP capital goods at 12.5% YoY — up sharply from 4.3% — is the most encouraging number in today's dataset, but core sector at 2.3% (cement, steel, electricity, coal) directly contradicts it: you cannot have genuine capex acceleration without core sector throughput. regime_classification.2026-04-09:consumption: {"run_date":"2026-04-09","dimension":"consumption","metric_summary":"GST 200640; PV 26.1%","signal_text":"GST at ₹2,00,640 crore crossing the ₹2 lakh crore threshold is structurally significant, but the Mishra question is whether this reflects genuine demand expansion or formalization reclassifying existing informal spend — two-wheeler sales at 28.68% YoY and CV sales at 28.89% suggest rural and freight demand is genuinely strong, not just a base effect. Passenger vehicle sales at 26.1% YoY confirm urban discretionary is holding, but airline passenger traffic at the 7th percentile and e-commerce GMV growth decelerating from 25% to 12.4% suggest the upper-middle consumer is pulling back. Retail mall vacancy rising to 5.7% from 5.1% while e-commerce decelerates simultaneously is the most underappreciated signal: neither physical nor digital retail is accelerating — consumption breadth is narrower than the headline implies.","badge_label":"Demand Surge","badge_type":"b-exp"}
regime_classification.2026-04-10:growth: {"run_date":"2026-04-10","dimension":"growth","metric_summary":"GDP 7.8%; PMI 56.5","signal_text":"PMI Composite at 56.5 flatters to deceive: Manufacturing PMI fell to 53.9 from 56.9, core sector decelerated to 2.3% from 4.7%, and airline passenger traffic dropped 9% to a 18-month low — none of which is consistent with a 7.8% economy firing on all cylinders. Invert the GDP print: the 170bps gap between the RBI's own forecast of 6.9% and the reported 7.8% demands a deflator explanation, not a celebration. What breaks this narrative is simple — if IIP capital goods momentum (up 12.5% this month but from a 4.3% prior base) doesn't sustain, the growth story reverts to its GVA skeleton, which is structurally closer to 6.5%.","badge_label":"Expansion Mode","badge_type":"b-exp"}
regime_classification.2026-04-10:inflation: {"run_date":"2026-04-10","dimension":"inflation","metric_summary":"CPI 3.21%; Fuel -3.78%%","signal_text":"Headline CPI at 3.21% is benign, but the composition is a slow-motion trap: food inflation has jumped to 3.47% from 2.13% in a single print, FAO Food Index is at 128.5 (a 2.5% MoM rise), and Brent at $96.44 means the fuel deflation buffer of -3.78% is arithmetically finite — one administered price revision ends it. Core CPI is sticky at 3.4%, unchanged for two consecutive months, which tells you demand-side pricing power hasn't broken despite the rate cycle. Munger's inversion: if RBI cuts into a food-inflation re-acceleration while Brent adds $15bn to the import bill for every $10 move, the second-order effect is a CPI overshoot toward 5% by Q3 FY27 — precisely when the rate cut transmission would be peaking.","badge_label":"Within Band","badge_type":"b-neu"}
regime_classification.2026-04-10:credit: {"run_date":"2026-04-10","dimension":"credit","metric_summary":"Credit 13.8%; CD 83.04%","signal_text":"CD ratio at 83.04% — the 81st percentile of its 10-year range — is the single most important number in today's dataset, and it is moving in the wrong direction from 82.5%. NBFC credit growth has already decelerated from 17% to 13% YoY, the classic early signal of wholesale funding stress; corporate bond issuance at ₹1.12 lakh crore sits at the 7th percentile — a decade low — meaning corporates are not accessing bond markets as a relief valve, they are rationing debt entirely. Historically, the last time India's CD ratio sustained above 80% during a rate-cut cycle was Q2 FY14; within four quarters, the taper tantrum forced 75bps of emergency RBI hikes — the setup today rhymes, except the external trigger this time is Brent, not Fed tapering.","badge_label":"Deposit Gap Stress","badge_type":"b-risk"}
regime_classification.2026-04-10:policy: {"run_date":"2026-04-10","dimension":"policy","metric_summary":"Repo 5.25%%","signal_text":"Repo at 5.25% with CPI at 3.21% implies a real policy rate of approximately +200bps — restrictive by India's historical standards, where neutral real rates have averaged 100-150bps. RBI's own inflation forecast has been revised up to 4.6% from 4.2%, which compresses the cutting room: a 25bps cut would still leave real rates above neutral, but the CD ratio at 83% means any rate cut that stimulates credit demand without a corresponding deposit response risks a macro-prudential response — higher risk weights on unsecured lending, as seen in November 2023. Home loan rates falling to 8% from 9.1% have already improved the affordability index to 0.61 from 0.46 — the transmission is working in housing; the question is whether the RBI can hold this line if food inflation prints above 4% in May.","badge_label":"Rate Pause","badge_type":"b-neu"}
regime_classification.2026-04-10:capex: {"run_date":"2026-04-10","dimension":"capex","metric_summary":"IIP CapG 12.5%%; CU Not available in search results%","signal_text":"IIP capital goods at +12.5% YoY looks strong until you note it follows a +4.3% prior print — the base effect is doing most of the work, not a genuine acceleration in investment orders. Core sector at 2.3% — covering steel, cement, electricity, and coal — is the most reliable capex proxy India has, and at an 18-month low, it contradicts the capital goods IIP entirely; cement dispatch and steel production don't lie about construction activity the way a single-month IIP print can. Capacity utilisation data is unavailable today, but auto and cement order book commentary points to plateauing utilisation — and Munger's question is the right one: if utilisation isn't above 75-80%, the incentive for private greenfield capex simply doesn't exist, regardless of what the budget allocates.","badge_label":"Steady Investment","badge_type":"b-neu"}
regime_classification.2026-04-10:consumption: {"run_date":"2026-04-10","dimension":"consumption","metric_summary":"GST 200640; PV 26.1%","signal_text":"GST collections at ₹2.006 lakh crore are a record, but Mishra's lens demands decomposition: at this level, a meaningful share of the YoY gain reflects formalization reclassification — informal-to-formal migration — rather than net new demand creation, particularly as e-commerce GMV growth has halved from 25% to 12.4% YoY, the 15th percentile of its range. Vehicle sales tell a bifurcated story: PV at +26.1%, two-wheelers at +28.68%, and CVs at +28.89% all signal broad-based demand — but airline passenger traffic at 35.08 lakh, down 9% and at a 7th-percentile low, suggests urban discretionary spending is softening at the margin. Invert the GST record: if consumption were genuinely broad-based, e-commerce GMV and airline traffic — the two most real-time consumption proxies — would not both be printing multi-year lows simultaneously.","badge_label":"Demand Surge","badge_type":"b-exp"}
regime_classification.2026-04-11:growth: {"run_date":"2026-04-11","dimension":"growth","metric_summary":"GDP 7.8%; PMI 56.5","signal_text":"GDP at 7.8% looks unimpeachable until you decompose it: PMI Composite fell to 56.5 from 58.9, PMI Manufacturing dropped to 53.9 from 56.9, and core sector growth collapsed to 2.3% YoY from 4.7% — the sharpest single-month deceleration in 18 months. INR at 92.59 (84th percentile) is weakening on idiosyncratic capital outflow pressure, not dollar strength — DXY is flat at 98.23 (43rd percentile). If FII outflows persist for another two weeks, RBI's intervention calculus becomes uncomfortable: defend the rupee and drain liquidity, or let it slide and import inflation into a food-CPI that is already rising.

Bank Nifty at 56,566 (93rd percentile) making cycle highs while the CD ratio sits at 83.04% and NBFC credit growth decelerates to 13% from 17% is the kind of divergence that historically resolves via a macro-prudential circular, not a soft landing. Real estate sales at 1,01,675 units — down from 1,08,970 — while unsold inventory jumped to 6,00,000 units from 576 units prior (a data anomaly worth verifying, but directionally consistent with launches outpacing sales) signals a supply-demand imbalance building in residential. Office vacancy at 13.9% (38th percentile), falling from 17.2%, and Bengaluru rents at ₹102/sqft (+9.4%) are the one genuine bright spot — commercial real estate is tightening on constrained supply, not demand exuberance. India VIX at 17.2 (13th percentile) suggests the equity market is not pricing any of these risks.

Three risks, ranked by probability × impact: first, FII outflow acceleration — -₹114 crore is a warning shot, not a crisis, but sustained outflows into a 92.59 INR print force RBI to choose between currency defence and liquidity support, and it cannot do both. Second, food inflation re-acceleration — CFPI at 3.47% from 2.13% in one month, with FAO Food Index at 128.5 (up from 125.3), means the RBI's 4.6% inflation forecast is already under pressure before the kharif season. Third, the US growth-equity divergence — a 0.7% SAAR GDP print with S&P at all-time highs is a setup that historically corrects via earnings disappointment within two quarters, and India's FII flows are the transmission mechanism. Watch Tuesday's RBI liquidity operation size — if it expands beyond ₹1 lakh crore, the deposit stress is worse than the published CD ratio suggests.
